summary: Spolsky argues that software companies should stop thinking of themselves as idea factories and start thinking of themselves as engines that convert capital into working software through talented programmers. The 'build-a-better-mousetrap' model fails because ideas are rarely unique, patents rarely protect you, and VC money chases easy-to-copy concepts. Microsoft's dominance came not from innovation but from its superior ability to recruit and retain great programmers who could outproduce competitors. Companies like Netscape, Lotus, and WordPerfect died when they lost this conversion ability. The strategic implication is that recruiting the right people should be a software company's number one priority, above all else.
key_points: The 'better mousetrap' theory of software startups is wrong — most successful software companies like Microsoft and Oracle didn't succeed through novel ideas but through superior execution.
Patent protection is nearly useless for software companies, and VC money gravitates toward easy-to-copy ideas, creating dozens of identical competitors.
Microsoft's real competitive advantage was its ability to recruit smart college graduates, retain them with stock options, and convert their productivity into shipping software at scale.
Companies die when they lose the ability to convert capital into code — Netscape stopped shipping, WordPerfect insisted on assembler, and both became footnotes.
Software companies should think of themselves as factories converting talent into code, the way restaurants convert chefs and ingredients into dining experiences.
money_quotes: The real goal for software companies should be converting capital into software that works.
I can hardly think of a single case of a company successfully protecting themselves from competitors because of a patent.
A software company has to think of recruiting the right people as its number one problem.
Hire smart people, and they will produce good stuff that you can sell and make money off. Then everything else follows.
It's amazing how many companies – Lotus, Netscape, WordPerfect – have bitten the dust because they lost the ability to convert capital into code.
Most startups seem to think that hiring good programmers is about as important as, say, getting the right PR firm.
If you want to be the number one restaurant in town, you have to worry about how to get the best chefs and the best ingredients.
key_insight: A software company's primary strategic challenge is not finding a unique idea but building an organization that efficiently converts capital into working software through talented programmers.
